title: Analytics Engineering with dbt + BigQuery
summary: A layered dbt workflow on BigQuery that turns raw customer data into tested, documented, analytics-ready models — staging, intermediate, and marts with data quality enforced in CI.
problem: Raw customer data in the warehouse was queried ad hoc: every analyst rewrote the same cleaning logic, definitions drifted between reports, and nothing was tested or documented. The result was slow analysis and metrics nobody fully trusted.
solution: I implemented an analytics engineering workflow with dbt Cloud on BigQuery. Source data is declared and freshness-checked, staging models standardize naming and types one-to-one with sources, intermediate models encode reusable business logic, and final mart models serve reporting. Schema and data tests (unique, not-null, relationships, accepted values) run on every build, and dbt docs generate a browsable lineage graph and column-level documentation.
impact: Analysts now build on tested, documented models instead of raw tables — shared definitions ended metric drift, failing tests catch bad data before it reaches reports, and the lineage graph makes the entire transformation flow self-explaining for new contributors.

architecture.description: BigQuery holds raw source datasets. dbt structures transformation in three layers: staging (cleaning and standardization), intermediate (business logic), and marts (analytics-ready tables for reporting). dbt tests enforce quality contracts at each layer, and generated documentation exposes lineage from source to mart.

challenges: Drawing the line between intermediate logic and mart logic so models stay reusable without becoming a tangle of dependencies.
Choosing materializations: views for cheap staging layers, tables for marts that reports hit repeatedly.
Making tests meaningful — testing what can actually break (keys, relationships, accepted values) rather than testing for coverage's sake.
designDecisions: Strict staging convention (one staging model per source table, renaming and casting only) — it keeps the messy edge of the warehouse contained in one layer.
Tests as contracts between layers: a mart can trust its inputs because the layer below enforces them.
Documentation generated from code, not maintained beside it — docs that drift from reality are worse than none.
lessonsLearned: Analytics engineering is mostly software engineering: version control, tests, and review do more for data quality than any tool feature.
Naming conventions are leverage — a predictable model structure means anyone can find and extend the right model without asking.
